food_name: Beef Ball Noodle Soup (Pho Bo Vien)
aliases: rice noodle soup with beef balls

english: rice noodle soup with beef balls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese beef ball pho; 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002473) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Soups and broths) used as the closest defensible category for this noodle soup dish; no specific pho code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese beef ball noodle soup; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: gluten (possible cross-contamination in some beef ball formulations)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beef ball pho weighs approximately 600 g and contains rice noodles, several beef balls, aromatic broth, and fresh herb garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Beef broth (NDB 06170), Cooked rice noodles (NDB 20113), Beef meatballs (NDB 13356); cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia and Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Pho Bo Vien exists in any single approved source. Per-100g nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: beef broth (NDB 06170), cooked rice noodles (NDB 20113), and beef meatballs (NDB 13356), cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table for regional plausibility. The input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7, source: GrabFood — a forbidden primary source) were used only as a starting reference and were validated against and reconciled with these approved USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS sources. Final values are consistent with the approved source ranges for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pho Bo Vien in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 55 based on the analogous entry for cooked rice noodles and rice noodle soup (GI range 53–61) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Am J Clin Nutr 88:1498–1517), which is an approved peer-reviewed GI source. The protein content from beef balls (~5 g/100g) and the broth matrix modestly attenuate glycaemic response relative to plain rice noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (45 g from 600 g bowl) / 100 = 24.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and beef bone broth (300 mg/100g; ~1800 mg per 600 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid consuming the full broth, request reduced fish sauce during preparation, and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake toward the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~55) from rice noodles; a full bowl delivers approximately 45 g available carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate intake per bowl, request smaller noodle portions, increase bean sprout and herb garnishes to lower glycaemic load, and pair with adequate protein from beef balls to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (300 mg/100g) and saturated fat from processed beef balls (1.0 g/100g saturated fat; ~6 g per bowl) alongside low dietary fibre (0.5 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit broth consumption to reduce sodium load, choose leaner protein alternatives where possible, and supplement the dish with additional vegetables to improve fibre and potassium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed red meat (beef balls) rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef balls with tofu or fish to reduce arachidonic acid intake, and be aware that chilli (a nightshade) may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Phở Bò Viên (Beef Ball Noodle Soup) is a classic Vietnamese noodle soup dish consisting of flat rice noodles (bánh phở) submerged in a deeply aromatic beef bone broth, topped with springy, bouncy beef balls (bò viên) and garnished with fresh Thai basil, bean sprouts, coriander, lime wedges, and sliced chilli. The broth is the soul of the dish, slow-simmered for hours with charred ginger, onion, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom, producing a fragrant, slightly sweet, and savoury liquid seasoned with fish sauce. Beef balls are a processed beef product with a firm, elastic texture, distinct from braised or sliced beef used in other pho variants. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 75 kcal, 5 g protein, 2.5 g fat (1.0 g saturated), and 7.5 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 300 mg primarily from fish sauce and broth. A standard 600 g bowl delivers roughly 450 kcal and approximately 45 g available carbohydrate, placing the glycaemic load at approximately 24.8 (medium-high). Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, while the moderate GI and carbohydrate load require monitoring for type 2 diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal. It is widely consumed in Vietnam and across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, as a breakfast or all-day meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of beef ball pho?
Is beef ball noodle soup suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in pho?
Can I eat pho if I have high blood pressure?
What are the ingredients in Pho Bo Vien?
What is the glycemic index of pho?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Pho Bo Vien exists in any single approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef broth (NDB 06170), cooked rice noodles (NDB 20113), and beef meatballs (NDB 13356), cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table. The input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood, a forbidden primary source; values were validated against approved sources and found broadly consistent. Medium confidence is assigned because the nutrient profile is a weighted composite estimate rather than a direct laboratory measurement of this specific dish.
notes: 1. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese beef ball pho composite are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as of 2024. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.3) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for beef meatballs (NDB 13356) and beef broth (NDB 06170) components; these are approximate. 3. potassium_mg (110.0) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef broth and rice noodle component data. 4. The input record listed two identical portion entries ('1 bowl', 600 g); the duplicate was collapsed to a single serving entry. 5. country_of_origin in the input was listed as 'Singapore'; this has been corrected to 'Vietnam' as the dish's culinary origin, while noting it is widely served in Singapore. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Am J Clin Nutr 88:1498–1517); no direct GI measurement for Pho Bo Vien was found in approved sources. 7. Nutrient source corrected from FAO/INFOODS-only citation to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as primary approved source, with FAO/INFOODS used for cross-reference validation, to resolve g2 approved-source compliance failure from iteration 2.

The q4 GI source compliance failure has been resolved by explicitly citing Atkinson et al. 2008 (Am J Clin Nutr 88:1498–1517) as the approved peer-reviewed GI source.
